title: Initializing Transformer Weights
concept: Careful weight initialization is critical; bad scales cause early layer activations to saturate or explode, killing learning.

options.text: The physical weight of the computer server.
The adjustable parameters that determine signal strength between layers.
The speed at which the model processes text.

exercises.explain: Weights are the internal parameters (knobs) that a neural network adjusts during training to learn patterns.

cards.title: The Starting Line Problem
cards.body: Imagine starting a race, but your shoes are glued to the starting line, or you are launched forward at supersonic speed. That's what happens to signals in a Transformer if we don't set the starting weights just right. The learning process fails before it even gets running!

cards.title: What is Weight Initialization?
cards.body: Weight initialization is the process of setting the starting values for the parameters (weights) in a neural network. In Transformers, choosing the correct range or scale for these starting values is essential so that information can flow smoothly through all the layers.

cards.title: The Block Tower
cards.body: Think of building a tower with building blocks. If the bottom blocks are way too big and heavy (huge weights), the tower collapses immediately. If they are tiny grains of sand (tiny weights), they can't support any height at all. Proper initialization is like choosing blocks of just the right size so the tower stands stable and tall!

cards.title: When Scale Goes Wrong
cards.body: If we initialize a Transformer's weights using a default scale that is too large, the values flowing through the attention and feed-forward layers quickly balloon. By the time we look at the first few layers, their activation values have saturated (reached extreme limits), making it impossible for the model to calculate gradients and update its knowledge.

cards.title: Myth: Training Fixes Everything
cards.body: Many people think that because computers are smart, the training process will automatically fix any bad starting weights. In reality, if the starting weights are too large or too small, the signals saturate or explode so quickly that the model becomes 'braindead' and cannot learn anything at all.

cards.title: Visualizing the Signal Flow
cards.body: Look at how the starting scale of our weights affects the signal flowing through the model.

right.text: Signals grow too large (explode) or flatline (saturate), freezing the model.

left.text: Signals stay stable across layers, allowing gradients to flow and the network to learn.

exercises.prompt: What is the main danger of activations saturating or exploding in a Transformer?

options.text: The model runs too fast for the computer to handle.
Learning is killed because the model cannot calculate useful updates.
The model starts outputting words in the wrong language.
The model automatically deletes its own code.

exercises.explain: When signals saturate or explode, the gradients (the signals that tell the model how to adjust) become zero or infinity, completely stopping the learning process.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Initializing a Transformer with extremely large weights can cause activations to explode.
exercises.explain: If weights are too large, the math operations inside the model multiply these values repeatedly, causing them to balloon out of control.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the warning about bad weight initialization:
exercises.sentence: Bad weight scales cause early layer activations to explode or ___ , killing the model's ability to learn.
exercises.explain: When weights are poorly scaled, signals either grow out of control (explode) or flatten out and saturate, which stops any learning from happening.

exercises.prompt: Which system shows a dangerous weight initialization strategy that will likely fail?

options.text: System A: Sets weights using Xavier/Glorot initialization to keep signal scales steady.
System B: Sets all initial weights to huge random integers to 'boost' the initial signal strength.
System C: Uses small, carefully scaled normal distributions for all layer weights.

exercises.explain: System B is correct because initializing weights randomly without setting a proper scale is highly likely to cause exploding or vanishing signals, stopping training. System A uses standard, carefully scaled initialization methods.

exercises.prompt: Why is careful weight initialization absolutely critical before we begin training a Transformer?

options.text: It uses less computer memory to load the model.
It prevents signals from growing too large or dying out, keeping training stable.
It lets the Transformer skip the training phase completely.
It guarantees the model will never make a grammar mistake.

exercises.explain: If weights are initialized too large, the math inside the Transformer layers will cause signals to grow exponentially (explode) or flatten out completely (saturate), which stops the model from updating and learning.
